# Transcrição: 01- Introduction to The Graduation Framework

## [00:00 - 03:02]

So now let's talk about how to build your tests, what goes into the testing process, and what my testing process is and why it's so important to everything that comes out of the BPM method. Now I use a system I call the graduation framework to test, to move ads and ad sets into optimization phase and get them ready for scaling, but at the same time building stability. And it's all about finding the right ads for the right audiences. Now my kind of analogy for this is imagine that you have an ocean of fish and you've got different fishing rods or nets to try and catch them and you're in your boat going around the ocean to try and find the fish. The way I see it is the fish are your customers or your leads or your prospects and the nets and the hooks that you're throwing in are your ad sets. So what you're trying to do in fact they're your ads and what you're trying to do is drop your ads into the right part of the ocean to find the right fish. So what essentially what I'm doing is to try and find the right mechanism to pull my leads or customers out of Facebook. So that mechanism is my ads and audiences and that's really what I'm trying to do at a minimal cost. So the way it works is with the graduation framework we've been through some of the core parts of account setup of an understanding what goes into the ad accounts that we create and the successful campaigns. So you've got your whole account setup which we've been through in the earlier part of the VPN method making sure your pixels and ad account are set up properly. And then we went through the core fall which is grading and analyzing where problems or opportunities are. Looking at your 5W avatar to build your audiences and ads. The full funnel system to understand how to leverage the opportunity of targeting the right person at the right time and bringing in the elements from the 5W avatar and then building that into your campaign. Now this part is graduation testing and now the graduation framework is taking those tests and then phasing them into your different campaigns and I'll explain what that means and how that works with insights into how I set that up in my accounts and then the step by step instructions. But the thing I want you to take away from this is that the process of testing and the process of researching your audience, your avatar, your creatives, etc. It's a structured process and this is really what creates the repeatable success. So when you look at some of the forums, some of the advice out there on Facebook ads, maybe

## [03:02 - 06:02]

even some of the advice from Facebook, it's kind of a lacking process. A lot of the reasons why some people run ads, they see some success and then they start struggling which increases stress and anxiety is because there's no repeatable structure and process. Now in our agency, we have this process in place and it's something which I've had to refine over the last 3-4 years to make it easy for everyone in the team to understand exactly the process that goes into creating repeatable success using my experience so that I can pass it on to others and they can repeat that level of excellence. So if you're running your own ads for your own business or you're doing it for a client, the same process is applied and it takes out a lot of the stress and anxiety of not knowing why something's working or not and it just creates more predictable success as well. Graduation testing is about dipping those ads into the audiences and essentially finding the winners. So the phase one of my testing process is to find winning audiences and what I mean by that is let's say you start with no data, you've got ad account brand new website, where do you start? So the first goal for me is to find good audiences and we've been through audience research on how to identify different kinds of audiences. The cheat sheet is in the earlier part of the training program to show you a wide range of ideas for different types of audiences. So lookalikes, interests, broad campaigns and everything in between as well. But when I start a new ad account, I need to identify a good number of audiences first. So generally I'm looking for three to five audiences that would give me a good two to three months of test runway. Then the phase two is finding lots of ads. So I've repeated this many times and I will continue repeating this. Ads fatigue faster than audiences. Your ads will have a shelf life and it will be shorter than your audiences. So if you find a good audience and not all audiences are of the same quality and not all audiences last the same amount of time, it's really important to understand that. So you might have an audience which is a 1% lookalike depending on which country you're in. It could be 500,000, it could be a million, etc. Now that 1% lookalike, there's two parts of it. One is the quality which is down to your custom audience and the other one is the competition that it's generating from whichever parts of the auction it's part of. The thing is that 1% audience could last a long time, especially if your budgets are

## [06:02 - 09:04]

low. But if you're really pushing those budgets high, chances are that 1% won't last. And then you'll need to create different audiences, i.e. go higher in your lookalikes to 2%, 3%, etc. But within that, often even with that 1%, just refreshing that creative can make a big difference. And the process that I use for graduation, framework and testing is aimed at giving you a ready supply of ads. So what I hate and what I find frustrating is you're running your campaigns and you see the ad is fatiguing and you don't have a replacement ad ready. I went through this when I was scaling heavily in 2015, we would often fall into this and now if I tell you we were spending 6 figures a day on Facebook ads, our ads were fatiguing very fast. So we were running global campaigns, e-com and our ads would have like 2-3 days worth of shelf life before we'd really see a dip in performance because we've pushed the budget so high we're reaching so many people. So we would have to refresh ads once or twice a week. On many campaigns, many ad accounts, you can go weeks, in some cases months as well with certain ads, but we were having to refresh quite frequently. And so we had to create a process of churning out ads consistently and that's where this whole idea of graduation framework and the whole testing process came about. I wanted to have a steady stream like a factory conveyor belt of literally just creating new ads that were proven before they hit my main campaigns. So what I mean by that is the advice from Facebook for example, at least from many of the people I've heard is when you're running a campaign and you've got good steady performance and you need to test a new ad, what Facebook say and what many people follow is just drop that ad regardless of if you know it works or not into your existing campaign. Now that introduces terrible, terrible risk because let's say you're spending $500 a day, $1000 a day on an ad set and it's performing really well but then you start to see the ads that are in decline. What happens if you drop a new ad in is it pushes the ad set algorithm to now think about I've got a new ad, it's a new variable I need to think about. I now need to push budget to that ad and I need to take budget from another ad. So you could end up with that new ad getting more budget but it could be a really bad ad and that bad ad could then bring your performance down for the ad set and create instability. So really what I'm trying to do is eliminate that as much as possible by pre-testing the ads with audiences that are already established.

## [09:04 - 12:05]

So in phase two we take established audiences and we are constantly testing new ads so that when I need an ad for my main campaign then I can just lift it from the testing once I've proven it works and put it into my main campaign which gives me a higher chance of maintaining stability and creating an ad that's going to have a longer shelf life as well. So that whole process is designed to keep your main ad account stable and I'll show you how that looks in the ad accounts and the process we go through in pushing ads into the process. And also what we do to identify what a good ad looks like in terms of the stats and we'll go through that as well. The way I look at it is phase one you have your campaign set up specifically to find winning audiences and like I said if you don't have anything you have to start somewhere so let's say you find three or five audiences you want to test I then pick ads and write ads that I try and test with the audiences and we'll go through that whole kind of research process. If I've now got audiences that I know work then phase two is now testing new ads to those existing audiences and then phase three which is my main campaigns and now working on stabilizing, optimizing and scaling and really there are only three steps to how I create my sustainability and stability on the accounts. Our number one is running the testing and I want to find stability by identifying the ads that work with audiences. It's important to understand that just because one ad works with one audience it doesn't mean it's going to work with another audience. So if I've introduced a one percent lookalike and you know ad A is working really well I won't assume that with a two percent or a five percent ad A is going to work I retest it and it's really important to understand that as well. Then I take those into my prospecting stage where I'm looking for stability. So by this stage in phase three I'm running those ad sets and audiences against the ads which have been proven to work in testing to see if I can maintain good performance whether it's a CPA or ROAS or other kind of metric then I look at scaling and so it's really important to understand the different steps that are going in and we'll go through that as part of the training here. Now you know one of the other things about this is I take advantage of something which is called discount bidding. So in Facebook the way this kind of works is that when you drop an ad into the auction and you drop an ad set into the auction Facebook doesn't really know how well it's going to perform. So the idea is that if what Facebook will try and do is give you a good part of the auction that it knows is high performing to see how your ad will perform.

## [12:05 - 14:16]

So here's an example if you go back to the algorithm so bid times estimated action rate plus user value equals your auction bid. What Facebook will do is give you an artificial boost. So if an ad hits the auction it gets a mini boost within the first 500 impressions what Facebook is doing is giving you slightly better chance of success in the auction because it wants to see how well your ad performs to its best audiences and by doing that you're actually maximizing your chance of getting good data so good ad to cart rates or sign up rates or you know purchase and stuff like that. What that means though is if that ad doesn't continue to give that same performance then then the ad can burn out. Now what I want to do is use that discount bidding mechanism to do my testing to get the best audiences to test so that 500 impressions and I'll explain how I use impressions and not spend to judge the success of my testing and what I want to do is test it with as few impressions as possible and then move it into my main campaign once I found it works once I'm confident that this ad really works with this audience pull it out with as few impressions as possible so that I can then rework the discount bidding in my main campaign and then put a higher spend behind it as well. So that's something which I'll explain as we go through the setup of the ads and kind of show you the data behind it as well but discount bidding is a really interesting part of how Facebook develop your ads in the auction to get an idea of how they work and there'll be a link below that you can click on to find out more about discount bidding. So essentially what I'm trying to do is the three phases of graduation testing find my winning audiences find more ads and then to stabilize my performance so they're the main things that we're going to be focusing on in this part of the training.
